title: Lucy Gonzalez -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist
meeting_title: Lucy Gonzalez -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist

transcript.text: Good morning.
How are you?
I'm doing well.
Thank you for asking.
How are you?
Good.
Sorry, I'm a little late.
Good.
Somehow Friday meetings are going long, too, but this should not take us 45 minutes.
So, yeah, happy to start.
I think you've chatted with Bridget and Tucker, as I understand it.
Yeah, that's correct.
Okay, so do you have a good sense of the role and kind of what we're looking for?
Yes, I did go over the roles.
I actually, I think Bridget was kind of in the spot where she thinks I fit with the...
Account director and account strategist role.
And she wanted me to have this conversation with you to get a second opinion.
I think the account strategist is a great opportunity.
And like I mentioned to her in the future, we do have sales openings.
And that is where I am put into and where y'all think I'm a best fit.
I'm more than happy to do that as well.

Part of my go-to-market strategies have been doing a little bit of everything from doing the working with customers, understanding their needs, aligning products, at the same time implementing them and making sure that they are using the technology and building out what they need.
So, again, whatever it is that's needed by the company, especially in this stage, I'm pretty scrappy and I'm pretty sure I can get my hands on it.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, why don't you like give me your resume, like talk me through your resume a little bit and like especially what you've been doing more recently.
Yeah, absolutely.
Well, I've been in go-to-market for
Around eight years, I started my career working at a hotel in the hospitality industry.
And then after that, I really wanted to start pivoting into tech and working in SaaS.

I got a job at Yeti, which is a consumer's packaged goods selling into large corporations for usually it was a seasonal job working in, for example, large Fortune 500 companies that wanted to give their employees certain products.
And then after that is when I pivoted into Logimata and really fully working with SaaS.
I started in sales development, and then I pivoted into the complete sales role.
But then after that is where I made the switch at Sprinklr, working more on the strategy part, implementation of the different technologies that we had.
I focused more on the social listening and publishing and building out queries for different brands.
But at the same time, I was focusing on understanding their needs, certain clients that I had.
I am...
And making sure that I had everything aligned for them to go and grow their company.
I was working with large enterprises.
I started working with enterprise companies over a thousand employees in size.
So sorry.
But then I was moving to the large enterprise, working with really large organizations like Johnson & Johnson.
Same thing.
It seems like I was working with really large organizations, but I was working with only specific departments.
Usually it was a research team, social media team, and understanding where can they improve, implementing the listening tools, and then continuing to build that relationship.
And then when renewal came, I would pass it over either to the renewal specialist, or if we didn't have certain people, I would also be responsible for that at times.
After that, I moved to HackerOne, which was my first step into cybersecurity.
The reason I made that change was I thought I could learn a little bit more into different industries.
I was working with...

A lot of companies sprinkler from biotechnology, e-commerce, retail, but I always wanted to understand also from the tech standpoint, how does these changes or how do these technologies fit within those different teams?
So I also started working there.
My focus was with the go-to-market team implementing and having a strategy on growing the brand in Latin America and also within South of the United States.

It was pretty straightforward what I did, but within each client, when they onboarded, they had to build a bounty program, and I was responsible of scoping that out, understanding what works to implement or keywords to put in there, attract different hacker talents, making sure that they were working within that project itself, and then aligning this with different teams.
So if we got success aligning that data with our different marketing teams, research teams, PR teams, if there were
Breach or issues, aligning that with the cybersecurity team to making sure things were patched and fixed.
And then after that, that's where I moved here into SEMrush, and I've been working here since then, similar role to Sprinklr.

I've been working with enterprise companies, but the difference is that I am completely managing the whole go-to-market for Latin America when it comes to building different queries around what products we need, what products we need to implement, how to prioritize different products, what languages are needed, legal requirements, and then the other part is implementing some of these technologies because we have around 60.
I tend to focus on the AI SEO part or AILM, GEO, different names, basically SEO for AI.
And building those prompts and making sure that companies are monitoring the right prompts, getting the right data for their needs, and then...
For the smaller organizations that do give me that permission, going and analyzing their content piece and seeing where they can optimize it to appear more in these LLMs.

Right now is a very interesting spot where SEO is still key and fundamental, but many people are focusing on how can we just improve visibility overall because visibility is making sure that you're visible across every single channel, not just organic or paid, but also in every single spot humans can be at.
So that's part of the strategy.
And then on top of that, I do have a, I wouldn't say it's a 50-50, but it's kind of like a 75-25 split.
I do also manage the majority of the south of the United States, so Texas, Florida, and those states.
And I do work with some of the largest companies around here and implementing, basically doing the same thing, going to some conferences, events, making sure that we have a good presence.
I mentioned District Bridget, but some restaurant now is in a spot where it is a very, it's a large.
Public company, but we're working for like a startup than anything.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
Well, I mean, it sounds like you do a lot of coordination across different teams.
So can you give me an example of an account you manage that requires like support from multiple players within the company?
Yeah.
An example would be this furniture company is called Jordan's Furniture.
I'm not sure if you're familiar with them, but they're in the New England area.
And we had a new VP of e-commerce.
The things that I had to navigate is working with the developers to make sure that the website was being audited.

After running an audit on my own, finding some of the biggest errors and comparing it versus the competitors, I sent that information to them and collaborated on what are the top issues to fix within their site to become that ninth.
They want to make their e-commerce the ninth store, if that makes sense.
They have eight physical stores, e-commerce.
course.
Of course.
The company is pushing for it to become the ninth store because they want to increase revenue without increasing the cost of a physical location.
So that's the top priority for them that I mentioned whenever we met is like, let me run an audit.

need to make sure that if technical is obviously, if e-commerce is your priority, how can we make sure that the technical part is up to date and up to standards for LLMs and also for Google, considering Google changes rules like every other month.
And then after that, I was introduced to the development team to go and navigate and speak to a bunch of engineers on what needs to be fixed on their website, what needs to be optimized.
To my learning, they only had like a few of them.
So we needed to make sure that those people were working on the top priorities, top priority issues, like new, no redirects, 404s, non-index pages.
Those were the top priorities.
Once those pieces started coming together, the other part was the marketing part, right?
It was how can we, once everything is optimized, how can we make

So I had to collaborate with the social media team, understand what technology they were using, they were using Dash Hudson, so how can we make sure that Dash Hudson is pulling data from SEMrush so they can go and create content that is relevant based on trends and based on actual data, not just like gut feelings, and we started strategizing around that, so I had to collaborate a lot with the social media team.
And then the last part is obviously the organic and paid ads teams.
Now, one of the biggest goal was how can we reduce paid ads but also increase that organic activity so you're found easier and at the same time reducing that cost so that I can invest somewhere else.
And that is where I worked with their SEO manager implementing SEMrush, the enterprise grade platform, so we can automate identifying some keywords and also I gave some the I do And
Feedback around content creation.
Because one of the biggest things that they were missing, they were not even preparing for this, I don't want to say season, but right now is huge in the New England area, football season.
And that was something that many other competitors were launching, creating blogs of like, look at our pillows, look at the table where you can eat and everything.
And that was something that they were not even monitoring or planning to do.
And that was something that we had to have a hard conversation.
If e-commerce is a top priority, we need to make sure that we're staying relevant.

Even if your clients are bougie, even if your clients are more high ends, how can we make sure we're speaking to these high end clients when a rug is $500, when something is very expensive, but still stay on trend?
Because your competitor like Bob's is taking that part of the market.
So that is something that they started creating more.
We started monitoring the competitors.
So at the
And right now we're in a spot where they want to continue doing that, but because they already purchased majority of the technology, now my role is we already strategized.
I already guided them.
My role right now is just can I speak to other teams like the BI team so we can connect APIs and see what products they need to innovate.
And that's where we're at right now because these other teams, my role right now is guiding them to go to professional services.

And because it's already a high-end client, we tend to manage, if they already purchased and they already like worked with me on strategy, then the job after that is seeing if we can collaborate with even more teams so they can go and work with the things that we already guided them.
So that was one of my clients.
We're still actually implementing a few other things for next year.
They're going to completely relaunch their sites right now.
We're in a very interesting interesting

Spot where they want to be ready for Black Friday, but at the same time, they have to make sure that they don't lose any credibility on their site because they're going to migrate to a whole new platform starting in January.
Wow.
Okay.
Awesome.
And then can you talk to me a little bit about like, that was a lot of moving pieces.
How do you think about organization for that stuff?
Yeah, I like to keep notes.
have a very extensive Excel document with all of the accounts I manage, and I manage a lot of accounts.
And so making sure that I know what part I'm showcasing them or working with them or what team I'm working with, and also what team internally I'm working with, because that is also as important.
Because if I'm pulling SEMrush legal, SEMrush professional services, those individuals internally at SEMrush is really important as well to make sure that everybody is moving in sync.
So I keep a spreadsheet of everything that we're doing, the accounts, also within our...
CRM, we have to keep extensive notes of what the plan is, the goals, challenges, and also ultimately what we're trying to achieve with them.
So it's a little bit of like CRM cleaning up and keeping everything organized.
But at the same time, I have my personal ambitious goal.
So I have a spreadsheet of how to organize everything and making sure that I have who I spoke and what team internally and also externally with the client.
And what are the next steps?
That way we're all aligned.
And also make sure my manager...
A spreadsheet for the, for like that is shared as well.
Yes, it is shared.
Okay, cool.
Well, we have something called like a mutual action plan as well with some high-end clients that are paying over 100 grand a year.
And those mutual action plans helps organize everything, what we're doing, what we need to do next.
Okay.
Awesome.
Okay.
Finishing.
Some notes.
Okay.
And then, so how many clients are you managing right now?
Right now, I'm managing around 100 in the US and 100 in Latin America.
But I will say not every one of them is strategic.
Like some of them are not a top priority.
They're like universities.
So I only focus on the top priority ones, which are around 25 in the US and 25 in Latin America.
Cool.
And how do you like structure and think about prioritization across those?
There's different ways.
First is just checking the data on the back end, understanding how many times you're going through our site and looking at the challenges of the business.
So we have AI tools to understand what the company is doing.
We also have like an integration with Gemini.
So it helps us prioritize which company should be a priority and which one isn't.
And then we also have ICPs or ICPs are e-commerce.
Thank you.
retail in financial services.
So we prioritize based on those three things.
One is based on data, current data, and then the other part is based on our ICP, where we've been successful the most.
Okay, and so like, how does the, how did Gemini and AI tools help you set priorities?
Like, what's the prompt?
What's the like, criteria that they're prioritizing against?
It's an internal, it's an internal prompt that we have to prioritize some of our accounts based on the integration between the CRM and Gemini.
Okay.
So what are the like, flags?
What, what, you know, ICP, obviously, I understand, like, you want to like, spend the most time with your ICP.
But then like, you know, you have 25 other accounts.
And like, how do you think about prioritizing over the course of a week?
Like, what are the flags that you're, that are raised?
What, like, how are you thinking about that?
Usually users and current expenditure.
Hmm.
So just, but that doesn't really change like week to week.
So how does that work?

Well, it doesn't change, but the information around the brands, if it's a publicly traded company, usually it does give us information if they're growing in certain sectors, or if they're launching a new product, or if they're in high demand right now.
So that data is pretty important.
That does change sometimes daily, but most of the time is quarterly.
Okay.
Oh, okay.
So it's pulling from public sources.
It's pulling from public sources and then cross-referencing with our internal data as well.
Okay.
That's helpful.
Yeah.
That's what I was trying to understand.
Does that show up in your CRM?
Uh, it shows it up in, um, inside Gemini.
It doesn't show it up in the CRM.
Oh, okay.
So it's almost like setting a Google alert for Gemini.
Right.
Got it.
But it does pull the data from our CRM.
It's non-bidirectional.
It's just one way.
Cool.
Okay.
Interesting.
Okay.
Well, can you talk to me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track and you had to kind of get the client back going in the right direction and what you did?
Yeah.

So the mutual action plans are actually in place for that reason, to avoid them going into a different priority or they leave the company, making sure that I have a second person to continue the movement.
And that's happened with Southwest, for example.
Right now, one of my leaders left.
So I have to reach out to whole new people and making sure that we don't lose momentum on creating the roadmap.
The roadmap is to be ready for AI in 2026.
And because we lost this person, we want to make sure that nothing is stalled.
So the mutual action plans is key.
But also keeping constant communication.
If they have an urgent timeline or deadline, making sure that they're working towards that is really important from the beginning.
Awesome.
Cool.
Do you have any other examples of like when something like, or maybe you can give me an example of how the mutual action plan works, aside from just like losing a point of contact?
Has there been instances where like the client wasn't meeting their goals and you had to change the, like how you're focusing or doing things?
Yeah.
Well, with Jordans, it's not going anything as expected.
One of the things we're having to pivot is monitoring every single location physically instead of just like working on digital.
And that is something that we share for them from the beginning.
It's like, look, if you're going to do everything digitally, you still have to take into consideration you have eight stores.
That reputation, reviews, and comments matter.
And those will hunt you into the digital world.
They didn't listen.
And now they're like, okay, well, you were, let's see what we have, can improve that?
And we're trying to find integrations to pull positive reviews and put it into their site so they know they're reliable in person, but also will be reliable digitally.
How have we pivoted and how would we make sure that, you know, if things go sideways?
Again, I've been working closely with the VP of e-commerce and I've been very transparent with them.
I try to be very honest with them that if they are going to go, it's kind of like going against medical advice, right?
You have to be aware that things will, may go perfectly for them or it may not.
So I was very transparent with them and we have constant communication.
An example was like for, they had like, when they had, Amazon had a, I think it's prime week or prime day.
I think it's Yeah.
October 7th to 8th, they had to try to have their own version, knowing well that they are a physical, they're known physically, not online as much.
They tried to have their own prime version of their furniture store.
And one thing that I would share with them a week before is that they should have told us that that was a goal like two months before.
So we can do the audit, we can have the keywords attached to all their main products, so we can have content created across social.
And mind you, they came to us the three weeks beforehand, and they're like, well, things are not going well, we're not getting a lot of attention to our website, like what is happening?

And at that point, we had to be very honest with you, how about we start working now, because this is going to be a loss, let's start working on Black Friday, because if we can't get ahead of that, and we do the same thing we did right now, we're going to be prepared.

And so right now, we've been working on that heavily, working closely to create content around that, monitoring what the competitors are doing and improving the base of that, and also creating content that is not just emotion-filled, but data-filled.
Like, it's backed by data, and it's things that are going to be trending and working for them.

So how we made sure that I was working is being very honest with them and realigning expectations, and then also making sure that I was going into their instances, because we can do that here, and just seeing what type of reports I can pull that we can implement right away.
So we can kind of shift and not have a complete failure, because three weeks before launching something massively like that, it takes a little bit more planning.
But it did happen with Jordans.
Right now, we're also still in a suite, in an interesting spot.
Hopefully, they're taking, we're taking everything that we have collaborated on seriously.
Because it seems like sometimes we are very serious about what we're trying to do and make online be the ninth store.
But sometimes because the stores do so well, it's like not a huge priority.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Okay, cool.
Well, those are all the questions I have.
What questions do you have for me?
Yeah, I think the only question I have is based on the conversation today.
What do you think would be a better fit?
Because Bridget mentioned it was a very interesting conversation.
And it was a good conversation with her, just trying to understand and grasp your thoughts and opinion.
Yeah, yeah.
I think you'd be a good account strategist.
So it's like account strategist slash we're thinking about it as an account manager.
I think you have a lot of like really good experience coordinating and working with clients like on a day to day basis.
And so I think you.
We'll do really well managing relationships closely and keeping projects on track.
So I think that's where I would put you, and I definitely would move you forward there.
I really appreciate that.
And just, I know the company, I think it launched in 2024, so it's fairly young.
From account strategists, what does the career path look like after that, if you have thought about that or if that has been implemented?
Yeah, so this is actually kind of a newer role.
We used to have, or we still, in some cases, have like managing editors and then account directors and account strategists.
It's like the newer account strategists are sitting between those two to allow for like smoother career pathing.
So account director, I think it would be the next step up.
And certainly, like with your experience, I could see the, you know, the ability.
I think
To move forward there, I think it's just would want to like kind of see you in action and in the org before I put you there.
Absolutely.
I appreciate that transparency.
Yeah, I don't have any further questions.
I think it was a great conversation straight to the point.
So I really like that.
Yeah, likewise.
Okay, well, Lucy, thank you for your time.
And I will talk to you again soon, I'm sure.
Thank you, Andy.
Have a great weekend.
Bye.
You too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Final interview to determine role fit for Lucy Castaneda.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=339.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Role Fit:** Andi confirmed the Account Strategist role is the best fit, citing Lucy's strong experience in client coordination and project management.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
  - [**Experience:** Lucy has 8 years in SaaS go-to-market, with a focus on enterprise strategy, implementation, and cross-functional project management.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=407.0)
  - [**Client Management:** Manages \~50 strategic enterprise accounts by prioritizing with an AI tool (Gemini) and organizing work with personal spreadsheets and formal Mutual Action Plans (MAPs).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)
  - [**Career Path:** The Account Strategist role is a new, intermediate step designed to create a smoother path to Account Director.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1697.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Experience

  - [**Career Arc:** 8 years in go-to-market, pivoting from hospitality to SaaS (Yeti, Logimata) before specializing in enterprise strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=407.0)
  - [**Sprinklr:** Focused on social listening and publishing strategy for large enterprises (e.g., Johnson & Johnson).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=456.0)
  - [**HackerOne:** Led go-to-market for LATAM and the US South; scoped client bounty programs and coordinated data across teams (marketing, PR, security).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=528.0)
  - [**SEMrush (Current):** Manages go-to-market for LATAM and the US South (\~75/25 split).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=606.0)
      - [**LATAM:** Builds product roadmaps based on market needs (legal, language).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=606.0)
      - [**US South:** Focuses on AI SEO strategy (prompt building, content optimization).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=606.0)

### Client Management & Prioritization

  - [**Prioritization:** Manages \~50 strategic accounts (25 US, 25 LATAM) from a total of \~200.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)
      - [**Method 1 (Data):** An internal Gemini tool pulls public data (e.g., product launches) and cross-references it with CRM data to flag high-potential accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1168.0)
      - [**Method 2 (ICP):** Focuses on core industries: e-commerce, retail, and financial services.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1194.0)
  - [**Organization:** Uses a personal, shared Excel sheet for tracking internal/external teams and next steps, plus formal Mutual Action Plans (MAPs) for high-value clients ($100k+ ARR).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)

### Case Study: Jordan's Furniture

  - [**Goal:** Make e-commerce the company's "9th store" to drive revenue without physical expansion.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)
  - [**Initial Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)
      - [**Technical:** Audited the site → collaborated with developers to fix critical SEO issues (404s, redirects).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)
      - [**Marketing:** Integrated SEMrush with Dash Hudson → enabled data-driven content creation.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=830.0)
      - [**Organic:** Used SEMrush to automate keyword research → advised on content gaps (e.g., football season).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=869.0)
  - [**Challenge & Pivot:**](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1400.0)
      - [**Problem:** The client launched a "Prime Day" event with only 3 weeks' notice.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1490.0)
      - [**Result:** The event failed due to lack of preparation (no audits, keywords, or content).](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1490.0)
      - [**Pivot:** Realigned expectations → shifted focus to preparing for Black Friday, emphasizing data-backed content and competitor monitoring.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1537.0)
  - [**Current Status:** Preparing for a January site relaunch while managing Black Friday readiness.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1009.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Andi:** Move Lucy forward in the Account Strategist hiring process.](https://fathom.video/share/8ApBsFWUJkMVzZ3s7ZXPojp6Sf5qYkEW?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)

action_items.description: Advance Lucy Castaneda to Account Strategist track; notify Lucy
